channel.title: NYT > U.S. > Politics
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Why The New York Times Is Expanding in Texas
item.description: Members of a new team of Times reporters and editors are covering culture, economics and more in a state long known for its political clout.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by Zachary Balcoff/The New York Times; Sandy Carson for The New York Times, Antranik Tavitian for The New York Times, Desiree Rios for The New York Times, Jordan Vonderhaar for The New York Times, Mike Osborne for The New York Times

item.title: Georgia Vexes Trump Yet Again: 6 Takeaways From Tuesday’s Primaries
item.description: Lt. Gov. Burt Jones lost the Republican runoff for governor to the health care executive Rick Jackson despite the president’s endorsement. Mr. Trump’s picks won in other races.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Nicole Craine for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s chosen candidates won three Republican Senate primaries on Tuesday, but he but ran into trouble in other races in Georgia.

item.title: State Lawmaker and Trump-Backed Pastor Head to House Runoff in Oklahoma
item.description: The result sets up a Republican primary runoff between a right-wing firebrand and a more traditional candidate.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner and Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Michael Noble Jr. for The New York Times; Mark Tedford Campaign
item.media:description: Jackson Lahmeyer, left, a pastor, and State Representative Mark Tedford will face each other in an Aug. 25 runoff in Oklahoma’s First Congressional District, a solidly red, Tulsa-area seat.

item.title: Trump Officials Challenge Reparations Program in Chicago Suburb
item.description: The Justice Department argued that the program, which seeks to compensate Black residents for housing discrimination, was racist and unconstitutional.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.media:credit: Anna Moneymaker/Getty Images
item.media:description: “It is race discrimination, pure and simple. And it is illegal,” said Harmeet Dhillon, the assistant attorney general overseeing the Justice Department’s Civil Rights Division.

item.title: Kevin Hern Wins Republican Primary for Oklahoma Senate Race
item.description: Mr. Hern, a congressman in the deep-red state, is now likely to be elected to the Senate to fill the seat of Markwayne Mullin, now the homeland security secretary.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Kevin Hern of Oklahoma had President Trump’s endorsement in his primary race for Senate.

item.title: Mike Collins, Endorsed by Trump, Wins Georgia Republican Senate Runoff
item.description: President Trump had made a late endorsement for Mr. Collins, who had campaigned as a MAGA candidate also able to appeal to moderates.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Audra Melton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Mike Collins has won the Republican primary runoff for Senate in Georgia. He will face Senator Jon Ossoff, a Democrat, in the fall.

item.title: Washington’s Reagan Airport Will Ground Flights for July 4 Events
item.description: The runways at Ronald Reagan National Airport will be closed after noon on July 4, and for several hours on July 3. Other celebrations surrounding the nation’s 250th birthday are also expected to cause disruptions.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian
item.media:credit: Kent Nishimura for The New York Times
item.media:description: Flyovers, parachutists and fireworks over Washington will temporarily force Ronald Reagan National Airport to occasionally ground flights between late June and late August.

item.title: Penny Brown Reynolds Wins Democratic Runoff for Georgia Secretary of State
item.description: Penny Brown Reynolds, a former judge, defeated Dana Barrett ahead of a potentially fraught general election to see who will succeed Brad Raffensperger as secretary of state.
item.dc:creator: David W. Chen
item.media:credit: John Bazemore/Associated Press
item.media:description: Penny Brown Reynolds, a former judge and senior civil rights official in the Biden administration.

item.title: Tim Fleming Wins GOP Runoff for Georgia Secretary of State
item.description: State Representative Tim Fleming defeated an ardent denier of the 2020 election results, Vernon Jones, in the Republican runoff to be Georgia’s top election official.
item.dc:creator: David W. Chen
item.category: Georgia Criminal Case Against Trump (2020 Election)
item.media:credit: Brynn Anderson/Associated Press
item.media:description: State Representative Tim Fleming.

item.title: 5 Things to Know About Jared Hudson, Republican Senate Candidate in Alabama
item.description: He is a former sniper with the Navy SEALs who is trying to spring an upset over President Trump’s preferred candidate.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Mike Stewart/Associated Press
item.media:description: Jared Hudson, a Republican candidate for Senate in Alabama, has put faith at the center of his campaign.

item.title: 5 Things to Know About Barry Moore, Republican Senate Candidate in Alabama
item.description: He was an early supporter of President Trump’s first campaign. Now, he has Mr. Trump’s backing.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Michael Brochstein/Sipa, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Representative Barry Moore, Republican of Alabama, has received support from the crypto industry.

item.title: The Crypto Industry Makes Its Biggest Midterm Bet Yet
item.description: The industry’s main super PAC has spent $9.8 million on ads to help the Senate bid of Representative Barry Moore of Alabama, a Republican seen as pro-cryptocurrency.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer
item.media:credit: Al Drago/Getty Images
item.media:description: Representative Barry Moore has voted for several bills that the crypto industry has prioritized this Congress.

item.title: Obama’s Nuclear Deal Looms Over Trump’s Iran Negotiations
item.description: President Trump is under pressure to significantly improve upon the Obama-era deal in order to justify the huge human and economic cost of taking the United States to war.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump campaigned against the Obama-era deal as far back as 2015, and ultimately killed it during his first term over the objections of many of his top national security aides.

item.title: Internal Emails Raise Questions About D.H.S. Ending Haiti Protections
item.description: Newly released documents could complicate deliberations by the Supreme Court over whether the Trump administration can immediately cancel deportation protections for some migrants.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo and Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Roberto Schmidt/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Guerline Jozef, co-founder and executive director of Haitian Bridge Alliance, in March. A Supreme Court decision on the Trump administration’s handling of humanitarian protections for Haitians is anticipated by the end of the month.

item.title: 5 Things to Know About Mike Collins, Republican Senate Candidate in Georgia
item.description: An immigration hard-liner, Mr. Collins has President Trump’s endorsement in the runoff.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Audra Melton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Mike Collins, Republican of Georgia.

item.title: 5 Things to Know About Derek Dooley, Republican Senate Candidate in Georgia
item.description: He is supported by outgoing Gov. Brian Kemp, who has had a tumultuous relationship with President Trump. Mr. Trump backs Mr. Dooley’s runoff opponent.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: David Walter Banks for The New York Times
item.media:description: Derek Dooley, a Republican seeking his party’s nomination for Senate in Georgia.

item.title: Inquiry Into Justice Dept. Lawyer Ends Without Disciplinary Action
item.description: A special counsel found that a government lawyer helped mislead a judge into releasing a man wanted for homicide abroad, but largely blamed the Trump administration.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague

item.title: Democrats Keep Helping Out This Far-Right Maryland Republican
item.description: Gov. Wes Moore, a Democrat, has begun running ads on Fox News that promote Dan Cox, a Republican running in a contested primary to challenge him.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Steve Ruark/Associated Press

item.title: When to Expect Results in Georgia, Alabama and Oklahoma
item.description: There are runoff elections in Georgia and Alabama and primaries in Oklahoma.
item.dc:creator: Taylor Robinson
item.media:credit: Audra Melton for The New York Times
item.media:description: A polling station in Albany, Ga., last month. In Georgia, Republican runoffs for governor, Senate and secretary of state are on the ballot.

item.title: Republicans Again Block War Powers Measure in the Senate
item.description: Four G.O.P. senators crossed party lines to back the resolution to direct President Trump to halt the war in Iran and seek authorization from Congress, but their support was not enough.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Michael A. McCoy for The New York Times
item.media:description: The vote on Tuesday reflected a determination among Democrats to continue aggressively pushing back on President Trump’s handling of the war with Iran.

item.title: Bay Area Voters to Decide on Swalwell Replacement
item.description: Eric Swalwell resigned in April as he faced sexual assault allegations, prompting a special election to fill out his term. Two Democrats topped the field in a regular primary election this month.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla

item.title: Hillary Clinton Says Biden’s Re-election Bid Was a ‘Terrible Mistake’
item.description: If the former president had “passed the torch” and allowed a competitive Democratic primary in 2024, she said in a new interview, the winner “would have beaten Donald Trump.”
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Dominik Bindl/Getty Images
item.media:description: Hillary Clinton and former President Joseph R. Biden Jr. have long maintained a relationship that is warm in public but intensely competitive and even resentful in private.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein

item.title: Justice Dept. Considering Death Penalty for Alleged National Guard Shooter
item.description: Federal prosecutors told a judge that they were deciding whether to seek the death penalty if Rahmanullah Lakanwal is convicted of killing a National Guard specialist.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Law enforcement responds after a shooting of two National Guard members near Farragut Square in Washington last year.

item.title: At Least 5 Charged in Alleged Plot to Attack White House During U.F.C. Event
item.description: Charging documents outlined an ambitious plan involving explosive-laden drones and rifles, but left less clear that the conspirators had the means to carry it out.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Ultimate Fighting Championship matches taking place on the White House lawn on Sunday.

item.title: ‘Are You Now or Have You Ever Been’ Review: Who Is Naming Names?
item.description: Anna D. Shapiro revives Eric Bentley’s play about the House Un-American Activities Committee’s investigation of the entertainment industry.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes
item.category: Are You Now or Have You Ever Been (Play)

item.title: Trump Breaks Up Education Dept., Prompting Worries Over Civil Rights
item.description: Special education programs and the civil rights office will be moved out of the Education Department, the most aggressive move yet by the Trump administration to dismantle the agency.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender and Dana Goldstein
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Education Department will shift duties for its Office for Civil Rights, which for decades has enforced anti-discrimination laws related to school children, to the Justice Department.

item.title: After U.S. Strike on Iranian School, Months Pass Without Answers
item.description: U.S. officials have not publicly acknowledged responsibility for the deaths or released a report on their findings from an investigation into the Feb. 28 strike.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Health care workers holding photos of children killed by airstrikes on the Shajarah Tayyebeh elementary school in Minab, Iran. At least 175 people were killed, according to Iranian officials.

item.title: Vance Is Pressed on Epstein, Race and His Boss on ‘The View’
item.description: The vice president also talked to Megyn Kelly on Tuesday about critics of the new Iran cease-fire and his presidential ambition.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Communion: Finding My Way Back to Faith (Book)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Vice President JD Vance’s book tour coincides with the administration’s drawdown of the conflict with Iran.

item.title: Takeaways From JD Vance’s New Book ‘Communion,’ on Faith and His Political Ambitions
item.description: In a new memoir, Mr. Vance recalls an “unsettling” meeting with Vatican officials on immigration policy and disavows his infamous disparagement of some Democrats as “childless cat ladies.”
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner
item.media:credit: Nathan Howard for The New York Times
item.media:description: In his new memoir, “Communion: Finding My Way Back to Faith,” Vice President JD Vance makes a running start on defining his political philosophy as the 2028 presidential race starts to take shape.

item.title: Iran Will Enter Nuclear Talks With the Trump Administration Feeling Emboldened
item.description: Despite military setbacks during the war, Tehran is presenting a narrative of victory before negotiations with Washington.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural showing Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, left, and Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, former supreme leaders of Iran, in Tehran on Monday.

item.title: What to Watch in Primary and Runoff Elections in Georgia, Alabama and Oklahoma
item.description: The top race of the day is in Georgia, where Republican voters will pick a nominee to challenge Senator Jon Ossoff, a Democrat.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Audra Melton for The New York Times, David Walter Banks for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Mike Collins has President Trump’s endorsement in his bid for the Republican Senate nomination in Georgia. Derek Dooley has the backing of Gov. Brian Kemp.

item.title: Gas Prices May Remain High Despite US-Iran Deal
item.description: The preliminary agreement may not have an immediate effect on prices at the pump. Damaged infrastructure and risky transport could keep costs up.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Jenny Kane/Associated Press
item.media:description: Gas prices have soared since the start of the war with Iran.
